data.title: Applied Digital initiated at Overweight due to HPC data center growth: CF
data.description:
Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) was initiated with an Overweight rating by Cantor Fitzgerald as the financial firm believes the company will become a large high-performance computing data center operator in the U.S.

The firm also assigned the Dallas-based digital infrastructure and cloud service provider, formerly known as Applied Blockchain, a $15 price target. The stock value has increased 33% over the past six months, but suffered a setback on Thursday when it closed down 8%. It was relatively flat during Friday market action.

Applied Digital is constructing an HPC data center in Ellendale, N.D. Earlier this week it announced the successful energization of its on-site main substation transformer at the site.

"This achievement will allow the Company to initiate equipment commissioning activities, bringing us closer to delivering the infrastructure we believe will define the future of AI and digital transformation in Ellendale and beyond," said Todd Gale, Chief Development Officer at Applied Digital.

"APLD is taking the 'if you build it, they will come' approach, as negotiations with 'Hyperscaler 1' have continuously been extended," said Cantor Fitzgerald analyst Brett Knoblauch, in a Fridat investor note.

Knoblauch said there is an 80% chance the first lease will be signed before the end of January. However, if "Hyperscale 1" ultimately declines the deal, it will take two to three more months before the lease is signed by a different hyperscaler.

In late November, Applied Digital entered into a promissory note with Macquarie Equipment Capital for a loan of $150M.

data.title: Consumer inflation expectations tick down in second December print: UMich
data.description: The University of Michigan's U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index was unchanged at 74.0 in December's second reading, up from 71.8 in November, as inflation expectations slipped further.

data.title: As Trump aims to slash federal spending, Wells Fargo economists see no major cuts in 2025
data.description:
President-elect Donald Trump upon returning to Washington will seek to jettison what he sees as excessive federal spending, and Wells Fargo economists project there will be marginal reductions next year with minimal impact on U.S. economic activity.

But tackling fraud targeted at the government may be worth some effort, they said.

Trump will step into the White House next month seeing that the government had spent $6.8T in fiscal year 2024 that ended in September. Wells Fargo Economics broadly broke down the figure to ~$4.1T in mandatory, or entitlement, spending, and $1.8T in discretionary spending. The latter undergoes an annual appropriations process, and Social Security and Medicaid fall under mandatory spending.

“We think some reductions in federal spending and employment on the margin are plausible over the next couple of years,” Wells Fargo Chief Economist Jay Bryson said, “but probably not on the scale that they will have large implications for a U.S. economy that has almost 160 million nonfarm employees and nearly $30 trillion of annual GDP.”

Overall, “we are assuming relative status quo for these areas in 2025, not major changes,” Bryson said in a research report published this week. Bryson, with Wells Fargo Senior Economist Michael Pugliese, noted it would take an act of Congress to materially increase or decrease federal spending.

Spending on Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, national defense and debt-service costs came in at ~$4.7T, nearly equal to the $4.9T in revenues the government had gathered. “It would take the elimination of all other spending, from veterans benefits to border security to education spending, to balance the federal budget if [other] spending categories are left unchanged,” Bryson said.

Wells Fargo said it’s “skeptical” about major-scale reductions in mandatory spending “given the enduring popularity” of many federal programs, chiefly Social Security and Medicare, the two largest.

Within non-defense discretionary spending, chopping down government headcount is unlikely to produce meaningful budgetary savings, they said. That’s partially because the $384B in estimated pay and benefits of civilian employees makes up less than 6% of federal spending.

But budget cuts are not impossible, Bryson said, highlighting that getting rid of fraud targeting the government could save hundreds of billions of dollars each year. The non-partisan Government Accountability Office in April said the government lost $233B-$521B annually between FY 2018-2022 because of fraud.

“Combating fraud is a challenging undertaking, but even if done successfully, reducing fraudulent payments to zero is unlikely to be enough to single-handedly put the federal government on a fiscally sustainable path,” Bryson said.

The U.S. budget deficit hit a record high $1.8T in FY24, with the government paying for it through the sale of Treasury bonds (US10Y) and other securities, stoking worries about bond oversupply. Trump’s new advisory group, Department of Government Efficiency, whose co-head is Elon Musk, is looking for overall federal cuts of at least $2T.

JPMorgan analysts this month said they expect aerospace and defense companies such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), RTX (RTX), and General Dynamics (GD) to face greater risk of federal budget cuts in 2025.

data.title: Netflix will broadcast the next two editions of FIFA Women's World Cup in the U.S.
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) and Fédération Internationale de Football Association have signed a pact under which the streaming giant has secured U.S. rights to exclusively stream the next two tournaments of the FIFA Women's World Cup.

The move further amplifies Netflix's foray into live streaming sports and events after it hosted the Tyson v. Paul boxing match and The Roast of Tom Brady. The company is scheduled to broadcast two NFL games and a halftime performance by Beyoncé this Christmas, and the platform is poised to become the home of WWE Monday Night RAW early in January next year.

The agreement for the Women's World Cup matches will include Puerto Rico and cover all languages. It will also feature a dual telecast for both English- and Spanish-language broadcasts in the U.S.

"The FIFA Women’s World Cup will be the first competition to be acquired in full by Netflix, which further reinforces the tournament’s status as the single biggest women’s sporting event on the planet and provides an outstanding platform to further promote the game...This is a landmark moment for sports media rights. As a marquee brand and FIFA’s new long-term partner, Netflix has shown a very strong level of commitment to growing women’s football," FIFA said on its website Friday.

In addition to the matches, Netflix will also produce exclusive documentary series in the lead-up to both tournaments.

The next two Women's World Cup tournaments are expected to take place in 2027 and 2031.

data.title: China Liberal Education Holdings announces share consolidation; shares fall
data.description:
• China Liberal Education Holdings (NASDAQ:CLEU) announced plans to consolidate 15 of its ordinary shares, each with a par value of $0.015, into one ordinary share with a par value of $0.225. 
  • The share consolidation will be effective on December 24, 2024. 
  • CLEU shares dropped over 20% on Friday.  
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: NOA Lithium appoints Carlos Pinglo as new finance chief
data.description:
• NOA Lithium Brines (TSXV:NOAL:CA) announced that the company and Dave Cross have mutually agreed to his resignation as chief financial officer.
  • Cross will be succeeded by Carlos Pinglo, as the new CFO.
  • Both Cross' resignation and Pinglo's appointment are effective December 19, 2024.
  • NOAL:CA shares were up 1.7% on Friday.  
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Rigetti gets initiated with bullish views at Craig-Hallum
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks have been receiving a lot of attention on Wall Street, with the latest action being from Benchmark, which raised D-Wave Quantum's (NYSE:QBTS) price target, and Craig-Hallum, which began coverage of Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) with a Buy rating.

Shares of D-Wave fell about 6% on Friday, while Rigetti was down about 2%.

D-Wave Quantum: Benchmark's Analyst David Williams said they were increasing the price target to $8 from $3 following their meeting with D-Wave's CFO John Markovich. The sentiment for quantum has clearly shifted over the last few weeks, with multiple industry and company-specific announcements helping drive the price inflection, he added.

The analyst thinks a recent capital raise eliminates the risk discount driven by much liquidity and dilution concerns, which they viewed as the largest overhang to the stock. However, with both concerns now cleared and the raise considerably less dilutive than potentially expected, the analyst believes the D-Wave story has become significantly more attractive.

Williams said he remains encouraged by the strength of the company's new programs in the pipeline and expect the cadence of deployments to accelerate as the new go-to-market strategy ramps up.

The analyst noted that D-Wave's go-to-market has historically been through its quantum-as-a-service platform and Professional Services business but did not have a hardware sales component. However, the company has become increasingly open to moving in this direction, which could generate high single digit millions in revenue per unit.

On the government front, the recent "Awardable" designation by the Department of Defense, or DOD, Tradewinds should help accelerate procurement and broaden access to D-Wave's platform, supporting quantum annealing adoption across the military complex, according to the analyst.

Tradewinds is DOD's platform for sourcing, funding, and developing solutions to challenges in the artificial intelligence, machine learning, digital, and data analytics space.

Williams believes that the company is positioned to benefit from the National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act over the next five years and sees room for upside if the firm gains traction in hardware sales.

Rigetti Computing: Craig-Hallum initiated coverage of the Berkeley, Calif.-based company with a Buy rating and $12 price target.

Analysts led by Richard Shannon said quantum computing has an opportunity to revolutionize the world like very few technologies can, and though its commercial impact is still years in the future, its market size and disruptive potential for classical compute makes it relevant to investors today. Rigetti has unique scaling advantages which may allow it to achieve equivalency or superiority versus its direct peers, and to achieve quantum advantage in the next few years.

The analysts added that Rigetti and other quantum computing names, in the recent weeks, have been caught up in a retail-driven fervor.

Although this environment creates risks for investors in the near-term that the analysts do not downplay, but they provided other perspectives, such as — the transformative impact of Al was not recognized by the markets until all at once in the spring of 2023, and investors seem to be expecting the next elemental step in computing.

In addition, the analysts said that even after this run up, the $1B to $8B valued quantum computing companies are still small compared to the more than $1T market cap classical compute-driven companies that could be disrupted.

The analysts also said that there is scarcity value in the public markets for quantum computing plays. As such, they think Rigetti and quantum computing deserve a place in investors' portfolios today.

Shannon and his team noted that classical computing is a huge market, measured in trillions of dollars, and quantum computing can displace some of it, while serving new problems classical compute cannot, which shows that quantum computing addresses enormous markets.

The analysts added that because of its potential to displace this large classical computing market, the potential for mergers and acquisitions by trillion dollar market cap companies that are threatened is real. As the most mature U.S.-based pure play superconducting quantum computing company, Rigetti is uniquely positioned to benefit from this type of interest, the analysts noted.

Shares of quantum computing related stocks have surged this year. Year-to-date, Quantum Computing (QUBT) has skyrocketed over 1,500%, Rigetti Computing (RGTI) has soared over 650%, and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) has gained more than 600%. IonQ has added more than 200%, while Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) has jumped over 125%.

data.title: Lowell Farms appoints Philippe Faraut as CFO
data.description:
• Lowell Farms (OTCQB:LOWLF) on Friday announced the appointment of Philippe Faraut as chief financial officer (CFO), effective immediately.
  • Most recently, Faraut served as CFO of iAnthus. Prior to that, Faraut was CFO of Irwin Naturals.
  • LOWLF is +9.92% to $0.0155.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow pare losses as core PCE comes softer than expected
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages pared some losses on Friday, after the November's core PCE report came in cooler than expected.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.5%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.9% and the Dow (DJI) was -0.4%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 5 basis points to 4.51%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 4.26%.

The Federal Reserve's favored inflation gauge - core PCE - ticked down in November, and edged up 0.1% M/M in November, less than the +0.2% consensus.

A stopgap spending plan backed by President-elect Donald Trump was rejected in the House of Representatives, further fueling concerns of a government shutdown.

"The big question now is whether a U.S. government shutdown is about to happen," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

The latest bill was voted down by 235 votes to 174. The funding is set to expire on Friday night.

"A short-lived shutdown affects government workers, but has limited economic impact. The longer a shutdown lasts, the more disruptive it is to the U.S. economy. Economic data may not get published in a shutdown," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

Wall Street's bounce back on Thursday following its worst session in over three months ran out of steam in the final hour of trading.

"For those wanting a quiet run up to Xmas, the good news is that there hasn't been any real follow-through to the Fed-induced slump on Wednesday. The bad news is that an initial recovery in markets struggled to gain traction yesterday," Reid added.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Affirm raised to Market Outperform as JMP calls it a 'long-term secular winner'
data.description:
JMP analyst David Scharf upgraded Affirm Holdings (NASDAQ:AFRM) shares to Market Outperform from Market Perform on Friday, calling it a "long-term secular winner at the expense primarily of the credit card industry."

Further gains are expected from continued adoption of buy now, pay later solutions for everyday payments, he wrote in a note, adding the company "benefits from a more robust loan platform relative to competitors."

Shares are already up nearly 40% since Affirm's (NASDAQ:AFRM) Q1 2025 earnings released on Nov. 7, though Scharf remains "constructive on the shares at current levels," based on prospects for a friendlier regulatory environment, as well as a faster-than-expected outlook for a return to GAAP profitability, better unit economics and user engagement, and momentum in new alliances and geographies."

JMP's Market Outperform rate diverges from the SA Quant system rating and the average SA analyst rating, both at Hold, but aligns with the average Wall Street analyst rating of Buy.

(AFRM) shares edged up 0.4% minutes after the opening bell.
